description: All ECG-related findings
Abnormal heart rhythms
Irregularly irregular rhythm without discrete P waves
Normal heart rhythm with regular P waves and PR interval
Chaotic ventricular rhythm — cardiac arrest
Wide-complex tachycardia >100 bpm originating from ventricle
Narrow-complex tachycardia originating above His bundle
Regular atrial rate ~300 bpm with characteristic sawtooth pattern
Sinus rhythm with HR <60 bpm
Sinus rhythm with HR >100 bpm — usually secondary
Ischemic ECG abnormalities
Acute MI with ST elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads
Acute MI without ST elevation — troponin positive
ACS without myocardial necrosis — dynamic ECG changes
Heart structural and electrical abnormalities
Thickened LV wall — meets voltage criteria
LVH with lateral ST depression and T inversion — STEMI mimic
Pericardial inflammation — diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation with PR depression
Prolonged QTc >450ms men / >470ms women — Torsades de Pointes risk
Type-1 coved ST elevation in V1-V2 — sudden cardiac death risk
Cardiac conduction system abnormalities
QRS >120ms with broad notched R in V5-V6 and negative V1
QRS >120ms with RSR prime pattern in V1-V2 and wide S in V6
PR interval >200ms — usually benign
Progressive PR lengthening then dropped QRS — usually benign
Sudden dropped QRS without PR change — high risk of complete block
Complete P-wave to QRS dissociation — haemodynamic emergency
Intermediate ECG signal findings used as derived-rule triggers — not standalone diagnoses
ST segment elevation detected on ECG — requires clinical correlation
ST segment depression detected on ECG — requires clinical correlation
Irregularly irregular R-R intervals — evaluate for AF or flutter
QRS duration >120ms — evaluate for BBB or ventricular rhythm
PR interval <120ms — possible pre-excitation (WPW)
T-wave inversion in precordial leads — evaluate for ischaemia or Wellens pattern
Premature wide QRS beat of ventricular origin — evaluate for structural disease if frequent
